displayTitle: Background, Dose Units, and the Word You Must Never Confuse

beats.narration: Before you measure a source, know what the world reads without one. Everyone on Earth carries a dose already.

payload.title: THE DOSE EVERYONE ALREADY CARRIES
payload.sub: Background is always there. Your meter never reads true zero.

beats.narration: Natural background runs about three millisieverts a year for the average American. Radon you breathe indoors supplies most of it.
Then the units. The gray measures energy absorbed per kilogram; the sievert weights it for biological harm. One sievert equals one hundred rem.
Now the words you must keep apart. Radiation is energy in flight — shield the source and it stops. It never makes you radioactive.
Contamination is radioactive material in the wrong place, delivering dose until removed. Sealed sources and six-month leak tests keep it out.

payload.sub: Sealed source, leak-tested every six months — radiation only.
